title: Eye Injuries: Recognition and Response
summary: Tell a minor eye irritation from a sight-threatening injury, and know exactly what to do — and not do — in the first minute.

sections.heading: Why eye injuries deserve their own protocol
body: Sports account for a large share of all eye injuries in teenagers, and racquet sports, basketball, and baseball are consistently among the highest-risk activities — a ball, elbow, or finger moving at speed can strike the eye faster than an athlete can blink away from it.

Most eye injuries in sport are minor: something blows in, or a poke causes brief watering and irritation that clears in minutes. But a small number are true emergencies, where the wrong first-aid move — even a well-meaning one — can turn a treatable injury into permanent vision loss. This unit is about telling the two apart and reacting correctly to each.

sections.heading: Quick anatomy
body: The eyeball (globe) sits inside a bony socket called the orbit, cushioned by fat, for protection. The sclera is the tough white outer coat; the cornea is the clear dome over the front that lets light in — it is one of the most nerve-dense tissues in the body, which is why even small scratches hurt intensely. The iris is the colored ring that opens and closes the pupil, the round opening in the center that light passes through to reach the retina at the back.
list: Sclera — tough white outer wall of the eye
Cornea — clear front window; easily scratched, very pain-sensitive
Iris and pupil — colored ring and the opening it controls
Orbit — the bony socket that houses and protects the eyeball

image.description: Side cross-section of a human eye seated in its bony orbit, with the sclera, cornea, iris, pupil, and orbit each labeled with a leader line. Use distinct colors for the sclera (white), cornea (clear, outlined), and iris (colored ring around the pupil). Clean medical-illustration style, neutral gaze.
image.alt: Cross-section diagram of the eye showing the sclera, cornea, iris, pupil, and bony orbit.

sections.heading: How eye injuries happen
body: Direct blunt trauma is the most common mechanism: a basketball to the face, an opponent's elbow or finger during a rebound, a racquetball or squash ball off the front wall, or a baseball taking a bad hop. Because these objects can be close to the same size as the orbit opening, they can drive straight into the eye rather than being deflected by the surrounding bone — this is exactly why racquetball, squash, and similar sports carry outsized eye-injury risk.

High-velocity projectiles — a line drive, a hit puck, a badminton shuttlecock — can also cause serious injury even without direct orbital contact, because of the speed and small contact area involved.
Less dramatic but far more common day to day: dust, an eyelash, or a bit of turf or dirt blown into the eye, or a fingernail scratch during a scramble for a loose ball.

sections.heading: Recognizing a serious eye injury
body: After any strike to the eye or eye area, check for the signs below. Any one of them means this is not a minor injury.
list: Vision changes — blurry vision, double vision, or any partial loss of vision in the injured eye
Severe or worsening pain in or around the eye
Pupils that are unequal in size or shape when compared to each other
Blood visible in the white part of the eye or pooling in front of the colored part (a hyphema)
The eye looks sunken, bulging, or an irregular ('teardrop') shape compared to the other eye
An object is visibly embedded in or sticking out of the eye
The athlete cannot open the eye, or the eyelid is deeply cut

callout.title: Any of these findings — treat as an emergency
callout.text: Vision change, unequal pupils, blood in the eye, an irregular or sunken-looking globe, or an embedded object are all signs of a possible ruptured globe, orbital fracture, or internal bleeding. These are time-sensitive: get the athlete to a physician or emergency room immediately. Do not wait to 'see if it gets better.'

sections.heading: Immediate response to a serious injury
body: The first-aid goal is simple: protect the eye from any further pressure or disturbance and get the athlete to medical care fast. Several common instincts are actually dangerous here.

Never press on, rub, or apply direct pressure to an injured eyeball — pressure on a globe that may already be ruptured or weakened can force fluid out and worsen the injury. Never try to remove an object that is embedded in the eye; pulling it out can cause more damage and more bleeding. If something is sticking out, loosely tape a rigid shield (like the bottom of a paper cup) over the bones around the eye so nothing touches the eye itself — do not use a flat patch that could press on the globe. Keep the athlete calm and still, keep both eyes covered if possible (covering the uninjured eye reduces linked eye movement), and get to a doctor or ER urgently.

image.description: Side view of a face with a rigid shield (the cut-off bottom of a paper cup) taped over the bony rim surrounding the eye, resting on the brow bone and cheekbone without touching the eye itself. Tape strips shown running from the shield to the forehead and cheek, well clear of the eye. Include a small crossed-out icon of a flat gauze patch pressing directly on the eye to show what NOT to do. Clean instructional-illustration style, no graphic detail.
image.alt: Diagram showing a rigid shield taped over the bony rim around an injured eye, resting on surrounding bone rather than the eye itself.

sections.heading: Minor irritation: what's safe to try
body: For something floating on the surface — an eyelash, a speck of dust, a bit of turf — a few safe steps often solve it without any medical visit: have the athlete blink repeatedly to let tears wash it out, or gently flush the open eye with clean water or sterile saline, tilting the head so water runs from the inside corner outward. Never rub the eye — rubbing can scratch the cornea or drive the particle in deeper.

If the object is easily visible on the white of the eye or under the lower lid and comes away with a moist, clean cotton swab or the corner of a clean tissue, that is reasonable. If it does not come out easily, stop trying.

callout.title: When flushing works and when it's the wrong call
callout.text: Flushing is great for loose debris. It is also the correct emergency response for a chemical splash in the eye — flush continuously with clean water for at least 15-20 minutes — but a chemical exposure still needs medical follow-up afterward, even if the eye feels better once flushed. Flushing does not fix an embedded or deeply scratched eye; those need a professional, not more rinsing.

sections.heading: When NOT to attempt removal yourself
body: Some situations look minor but are not safe to handle on the sideline. Refer for professional care rather than continuing to try home remedies when:
list: The scratchy, 'something's in there' feeling persists after blinking and flushing — this often means a corneal scratch (abrasion), not a loose particle
The object is embedded, will not come free with gentle flushing, or is on the colored part of the eye rather than the white
Light bothers the eye significantly (photophobia) or vision is affected
A chemical was involved — flush, then still see a doctor
There was any blunt trauma alongside the irritation, not just a foreign body

sections.heading: Prevention: protective eyewear
body: The best treatment for a serious eye injury is not needing one. Sport-specific protective eyewear with polycarbonate lenses — a shatter-resistant plastic — is the standard for high-risk sports, and its protective frame design (not just the lens material) is what actually keeps a ball or finger from reaching the eye.

Regular prescription glasses, sunglasses, or safety glasses bought off a hardware-store shelf are not sufficient: their lenses can shatter on impact and the frames are not built to withstand sports-level force, sometimes making an injury worse by driving broken lens material into the eye.

Racquetball, squash, and similar racquet sports, along with hockey and lacrosse, commonly require certified protective eyewear by rule; basketball, baseball, and soccer don't always mandate it, but athletes with a history of eye injury, an only-good eye, or who simply want to reduce risk are strongly encouraged to wear it anyway.

quiz.question: Which tissue forms the clear, dome-shaped front window of the eye and is extremely pain-sensitive when scratched?
choices: Sclera
Iris
Cornea
Orbit
quiz.explanation: The cornea is the clear front covering of the eye; it is one of the most nerve-dense tissues in the body, so even a small scratch causes sharp pain. The sclera is the white outer wall, the iris is the colored ring, and the orbit is the bony socket.

quiz.question: Why do racquetball and squash carry unusually high eye-injury risk compared to many other sports?
choices: Players never wear any protective gear
The ball is close to the size of the eye socket opening and can drive straight into the eye rather than being deflected
The sport is played outdoors in bright light
Matches last much longer than other sports
quiz.explanation: A racquetball is small enough to fit inside the bony orbit rim, so it can strike the eye directly instead of being blocked by surrounding bone the way a larger ball would be — this is why these sports mandate eyewear.

quiz.question: A baseball player is hit near the eye. On exam, one pupil looks larger than the other and his vision is blurry. What does this combination most concern you for?
choices: A simple eyelash stuck under the eyelid
Normal reaction to bright field lights
A serious eye injury — possible globe rupture or orbital fracture requiring urgent care
Nothing significant, since the eye isn't bleeding
quiz.explanation: Unequal pupils plus a vision change are red-flag signs of a serious eye injury and need urgent medical evaluation — the absence of visible bleeding does not rule out serious internal injury.

quiz.question: An athlete takes a finger to the eye and there is now visible blood pooling in front of the colored part of the eye. What is this finding called?
choices: Hyphema
Ptosis
Nystagmus
Photophobia
quiz.explanation: A hyphema is blood pooling in the front chamber of the eye, in front of the iris/pupil — it is a serious finding that needs urgent physician evaluation, not sideline management.

quiz.question: A pencil-like object is sticking out of an athlete's eye after a fall. What is the correct first-aid response?
choices: Carefully pull the object straight out so it doesn't move around
Apply firm, direct pressure over the eye to control bleeding
Tape a rigid shield over the bones around the eye without touching the object, then get emergency care
Patch both eyes tightly with gauze and ice
quiz.explanation: Never remove an embedded object or press on an injured eye — both can cause further damage. Shield the area with a rigid cover that rests on the surrounding bone, avoid touching the object, and get emergency care immediately.

quiz.question: A soccer player says a bit of grit blew into her eye during a drill. It's not causing vision changes and there was no impact. What is the appropriate first step?
choices: Rub the eye firmly to work the grit out
Have her blink repeatedly and gently flush the eye with clean water
Immediately transport to the emergency room
Apply a rigid eye shield and tape it in place
quiz.explanation: Loose debris without trauma or vision change is typically minor — blinking and gentle flushing (never rubbing, which can scratch the cornea) is the appropriate first response. Rigid shields and ER transport are for serious/embedded injuries.

quiz.question: After a chemical splash to the eye, the athlete flushes it for a minute and says it feels better. What should happen next?
choices: Nothing further — a minute of flushing is enough and the athlete can return to play
Continue flushing for at least 15-20 minutes total, and still see a doctor afterward even if it feels better
Apply a rigid shield instead of flushing
Only flush if vision changes appear
quiz.explanation: Chemical exposures need prolonged flushing (at least 15-20 minutes) regardless of how quickly symptoms ease, and still require medical follow-up — feeling better after brief flushing doesn't mean the chemical injury is resolved.

quiz.question: Why aren't regular prescription glasses considered adequate eye protection for a high-risk sport like basketball?
choices: They block too much peripheral vision to be useful in sport
Their lenses can shatter on impact and the frames aren't built to withstand sports-level force
They are more expensive than sport-specific eyewear
They are actually just as protective as polycarbonate sport eyewear
quiz.explanation: Standard glasses use lenses and frames designed for everyday wear, not impact — they can shatter and even worsen an injury. Sport-specific eyewear with polycarbonate (shatter-resistant) lenses and an impact-rated frame is the standard for high-risk sports.

flashcards.back: The clear, dome-shaped front window of the eye; extremely nerve-dense, so scratches are very painful.
The tough white outer wall of the eyeball.
The bony socket that houses and protects the eyeball.
Blood pooling in the front chamber of the eye, in front of the iris/pupil — a serious finding requiring urgent care.
Racquet sports (racquetball, squash), basketball, and baseball — small fast objects and close contact drive risk up.
Vision changes, severe pain, unequal pupils, blood in the eye, a sunken/irregular-looking eye, or an embedded object.

flashcards.front: Never do this to an injured eye
flashcards.back: Never press on or rub an injured eyeball, and never try to remove an embedded object — both can worsen the injury.

flashcards.front: Shield vs. patch for an embedded object
flashcards.back: Use a rigid shield taped over the surrounding bone (not the eye itself); a flat patch can press on the globe and cause harm.

flashcards.front: Safe self-care for loose debris in the eye
flashcards.back: Blink repeatedly and gently flush with clean water or saline, from the inside corner outward — never rub.
Flush continuously with clean water for at least 15-20 minutes, then still see a doctor even if symptoms improve.

flashcards.front: When irritation is NOT safe to self-treat
flashcards.back: Persistent scratchy feeling after flushing, embedded object, object on the colored part of the eye, light sensitivity, vision change, or any trauma involved.
Shatter-resistant plastic used in sport-specific protective eyewear; regular glasses lenses and frames are not built for impact.
